title: Buy local to build Aussie AI industry
full_text:
Start-ups Businesses are being urged to procure services from home-grown outfits to develop more national expertise, writes Alexandra Cain.
Australia must take control of its digital future, paying special attention to data sovereignty, or risk being left behind in the artificial intelligence arms race. This starts with making it easier to buy services from early-stage, local AI firms.
"How Australia navigates this digital transition will be a primary driver of our success in the 21st century," says Andrew Charlton, Assistant Minister for Science, Technology and the Digital Economy. "If we as a nation position ourselves to profit in the global digital economy, expand our productivity, increase our growth to equip our workforce, we will remain among the first rank of global nations by economic prosperity." Businesses are being urged to procure AI services from Aussie outfits to develop more national expertise in this sector.
Many Australian corporations buy AI software and related services from offshore firms because of a dearth of local companies with the skills and expertise. But that is expected to rapidly change, with a boom in AI-related patents and research.
"If we buy foreign technology [and] don't give Australian companies a go, we will make ourselves permanent renters of the intelligence of the 21st century," says Charlton.
Building the local AI sector's muscle is especially important, given the massive support Australia's competitors across Asia Pacific give their AI start-ups. South Korea is investing $US102 billion ($141 billion) in a National Growth Fund to supercharge its AI ecosystem and gives generous R&D tax concessions to AI start-ups. Changes to the local R&D regime are on the cards, so Australia can compete in global markets.
"We're producing a lot of the talent that is required. Where is our advantage going to be?" says UNSW AI Institute director Sue Keay. "I think it is on how we ... back our ability to build those AI models here onshore, as opposed to relying [on] and opening up our data sets to foreign companies. I think that is where you're going to get your competitive advantage."
The decisions local firms make about their AI service providers will help shape the tech sector's future. "CEOs have pitch decks on their desks. Procurement officers have RFQs out right now," says Charlton. "Every organisation is thinking about how to incorporate AI into their business and I want to make sure that there is an Australian business on that list.
"Because if we keep saying, 'oh well, the Australian business didn't have as much track record as the foreign business', then we will make that true. But if we give those Australian businesses a go ... some of the incredible Australian firms that we have ... will have the track record to get that second customer, to get that third customer and build an AI industry in Australia."
As this happens Australia must make sure its AI backbone remains here. "You want to grab hold of the physical infrastructure. So the data centres, the chips, the water, the electricity, that sort of thing," says Elanor Huntington, deputy chief executive of the CSIRO. "You want to grab hold of the data. You want to govern it well and have skilled people who can run it and train it and develop it further."AFR
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: Data Science and AI
